findings.title: disconnectAll silently swallows failures after clearing clients map
findings.description: When disconnectAll() encounters failures, it detects them (line 48-49) but only calls this.clients.clear() to force-clean the map. The failure reasons are never logged, never thrown, and never returned to the caller. The caller (McpHub.shutdown) has no way to know that some MCP server connections may still be alive as zombie child processes. This is especially problematic for stdio transports where a failed close means the child process is still running.

findings.title: removeServer catch block silently swallows disconnect errors
findings.code_snippet:
try { await this.clientManager.disconnect(id) }
catch { /* proceed with removal even if disconnect fails */ }
findings.description: The catch block in removeServer has a comment explaining intent (proceed with removal) but the error parameter is not even bound. This means: (1) No logging of the failed disconnect -- operators cannot diagnose why the disconnect failed, (2) For stdio transports, a failed disconnect could leave a zombie child process, (3) The comment documents intent but does not constitute handling. Per CLAUDE.md error_handling rules: 'Catch -> Log with context -> Re-throw typed error or handle gracefully'. A bare catch with only a comment violates this.

findings.title: aggregateTools loop has no error handling for individual server failures
findings.description: The aggregateTools method iterates over all connected clients and calls client.tools() sequentially. If any single server fails (network timeout, process crash, protocol error), the entire aggregation aborts with an unhandled exception. This means: (1) One flaky server prevents tool discovery from all servers, (2) The caller (McpHub.aggregateTools) has no per-server error granularity, (3) For a hub managing multiple servers, this is a single-point-of-failure pattern. This is a network I/O operation without try/catch protection.

findings.title: Transport cleanup catch block lacks error binding and logging
findings.code_snippet:
try { await (transport as { close: () => Promise<void> }).close() }
catch { /* best-effort cleanup */ }
findings.description: Inside the connect() error path, when the MCP client creation fails, the code attempts to close the transport as best-effort cleanup. The catch block swallows the cleanup error entirely without binding the error parameter or logging. While best-effort cleanup is a reasonable pattern, silently swallowing the cleanup error means: (1) If the transport is a stdio process, a failed close could leave a zombie process with no diagnostic trail, (2) The outer catch on line 30 will throw the original error, so the cleanup failure is completely invisible. This is not a P0 (it's a cleanup path, not primary logic) but warrants at least a debug-level log.

findings.title: createTransport has no error handling for StdioMCPTransport construction
findings.description: The createTransport factory function constructs an Experimental_StdioMCPTransport without any error handling. If the constructor throws (e.g., command not found on PATH, permission denied, invalid args format), the raw library error propagates with no additional context about which MCP server config caused the failure. The caller (McpClientManager.connect) does wrap this in a try/catch, but the error message from the SDK constructor may lack the server ID/name context needed for debugging. This is mitigated by the fact that connect() wraps it with config.id in the error message, making this a P2 rather than P1.

findings.title: connectEnabled could use Result pattern for type-safe error reporting
findings.description: The connectEnabled method already returns a ConnectEnabledResult with connected/failed arrays, which is a good pattern for partial-failure reporting. However, the failed array uses { id: string; error: string } which loses the original Error object (including cause chain). This is an informational suggestion -- the current implementation is functional but could be improved by preserving error instances or using a more structured Result/Either pattern from the Functional Core architecture guidelines.
findings.suggestion:
Consider preserving the Error instance in the failed array for programmatic error inspection by callers:

```typescript
export interface ConnectEnabledResult {
  connected: string[]
  failed: Array<{ id: string; error: string; cause?: unknown }>
}
```

Or use a discriminated union Result type for each connection attempt.

positive_observations: Constructor resource cleanup: McpServerStore constructor properly closes the database in the catch block if init fails (lines 27-35), preventing resource leaks.
JSON.parse protected: safeJsonParse function wraps JSON.parse with try/catch and includes contextual error messages including the server ID and a truncated preview of the corrupt data.
Shutdown uses try/finally: McpHub.shutdown() properly uses try/finally to ensure store.close() runs even if disconnectAll() throws -- this addresses a prior review finding.
Error wrapping with cause: McpClientManager.connect() wraps errors with new Error(msg, { cause: err }), preserving the original error chain for debugging.

Promise.allSettled for batch operations: Both disconnectAll() and connectEnabled() use Promise.allSettled instead of Promise.all, preventing fail-fast behavior on partial failures -- this addresses a prior review finding.
Input validation is thorough: validateConfig checks transport-specific required fields, command allowlist, and shell metacharacter injection -- solid defense-in-depth.
Parameterized SQL queries: All SQL statements use parameterized queries with '?' placeholders, preventing SQL injection.
